socialHistory.alcohol: Moderate — 3-4 glasses of wine per week
socialHistory.notes: HR Director. Single, no children. Close with mother (breast cancer survivor, BRCA2+). Handles her health with a project-management approach. Emotionally composed but BRCA2 status is a source of underlying anxiety.

familyHistory.condition: Breast cancer diagnosed at age 47, BRCA2 positive, bilateral mastectomy, currently in remission
Breast cancer at age 52, ovarian cancer at age 61, deceased at age 63

code.display: Genetic susceptibility to malignant neoplasm of breast

conditions.notes: BRCA2 pathogenic variant confirmed via genetic testing (March 2019). Patient referred to genetic counseling at Northwestern; initial counseling completed April 2019. Enhanced screening protocol initiated: annual mammogram since age 30, alternating breast MRI every 6 months. Discussed risk-reducing surgical options; patient currently elects enhanced surveillance. Annual CA-125 for ovarian cancer screening given family history.

code.display: Migraine with aura, not intractable, without status migrainosus

conditions.notes: Episodic migraines with visual aura (scintillating scotoma, ~20 min prodrome). Initially 4 episodes per month. Started topiramate prophylaxis in 2023, reduced frequency to approximately 1 per month. Uses sumatriptan 50mg PRN for breakthrough. Common triggers: stress, disrupted sleep, red wine. Aura presence noted — oral contraceptives with estrogen contraindicated due to stroke risk.

code.display: Sumatriptan 50 MG Oral Tablet

medications.frequency: As needed for acute migraine, max 200 mg/day

code.display: Topiramate 50 MG Oral Tablet

medications.notes: Started for migraine prophylaxis. Titrated from 25 mg to 50 mg over 4 weeks. Reduced migraine frequency from ~4/month to ~1/month. Patient tolerating well; mild paresthesias in fingertips reported initially, resolved.

code.display: Norgestimate 0.25 MG / Ethinyl Estradiol 0.035 MG Oral Tablet

medications.stopReason: Discontinued due to migraine with aura diagnosis. Estrogen-containing oral contraceptives contraindicated per WHO category 4 (increased stroke risk with migraine with aura). Switched to norethindrone 0.35 mg progestin-only pill.

code.display: Norethindrone 0.35 MG Oral Tablet

encounters.reasonForVisit: Annual physical exam and BRCA2 screening protocol review

notes.subjective: Patient presents for annual physical. Reports feeling well overall. Migraines currently averaging 3-4 per month with visual aura, lasting 4-6 hours. Triggers include work stress and disrupted sleep. Using sumatriptan PRN with good relief. Interested in discussing prophylactic medication. No breast complaints. Adherent to screening protocol.
notes.objective: Well-appearing 40-year-old female in no acute distress. HEENT normal. Heart regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Lungs clear bilaterally. Breast exam: no masses, no skin changes, no axillary lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, non-tender. Skin: no concerning lesions. Neurological exam normal.
notes.assessment: 1. BRCA2 carrier (Z15.01) — adherent to enhanced screening protocol. Annual mammogram completed May 2023, BIRADS 1 (negative). Next breast MRI due November 2023. 2. Migraine with aura (G43.109) — frequency increasing, 3-4/month. Candidate for prophylactic therapy. 3. Health maintenance: up to date on immunizations, routine labs ordered.
notes.plan: 1. Continue enhanced breast cancer screening: mammogram annually, breast MRI alternating every 6 months. CA-125 annually for ovarian screening. 2. Discuss migraine prophylaxis options — recommend starting topiramate 25 mg daily, titrate to 50 mg in 2 weeks. Continue sumatriptan 50 mg PRN for breakthrough. 3. Order annual labs: CBC, CMP, lipid panel. 4. Follow up in 2 months to assess topiramate response.

referrals.reason: BRCA2 enhanced screening protocol, alternating with mammogram every 6 months

encounters.reasonForVisit: Migraine prophylaxis follow-up and topiramate initiation

notes.subjective: Patient reports migraines have continued at 3-4 per month since last visit. She has been tracking them in a spreadsheet: 3 in July, 4 in August so far. Most associated with visual aura (scintillating scotoma lasting ~20 min), followed by unilateral throbbing headache, photophobia, and nausea. Sumatriptan provides relief within 1-2 hours. She is ready to start prophylactic medication. Reports mild fingertip tingling she read about as a topiramate side effect — wants to discuss before starting.
notes.objective: Well-appearing, alert, in no distress. Vital signs within normal limits. Neurological exam: cranial nerves II-XII intact, no focal deficits, no papilledema on fundoscopic exam. No signs of medication adverse effects (not yet started).
notes.assessment: Migraine with aura (G43.109) — frequency 3-4/month, significantly impacting quality of life and work productivity. Initiating prophylactic therapy.
notes.plan: 1. Start topiramate 25 mg PO daily at bedtime for 2 weeks, then increase to 50 mg daily. Counseled on common side effects: paresthesias, weight changes, cognitive slowing, kidney stones (increase fluid intake). 2. Continue sumatriptan 50 mg PRN for breakthrough migraines. 3. Migraine diary: patient to continue tracking frequency, triggers, and severity. 4. Follow up in 3 months to assess prophylaxis response. 5. Reminded: estrogen-containing contraceptives remain contraindicated; continue norethindrone.

encounters.reasonForVisit: Migraine prophylaxis 5-month check and breast MRI results review

notes.subjective: Patient reports significant improvement in migraine frequency since starting topiramate 50 mg daily. Migraines reduced from 3-4/month to approximately 1/month. Mild paresthesias in fingertips during first month, now resolved. No cognitive side effects. Weight stable. Breast MRI (November 2023) returned negative — patient reviewed results online and wants to confirm. Emotionally doing well. Inquiring about genetic counselor follow-up to discuss prophylactic surgery options 'when the time comes.'
notes.objective: Well-appearing 41-year-old female. Vital signs normal. Breast exam unremarkable. Neurological exam normal. Reviewed breast MRI report: BIRADS 1, no suspicious enhancement, no masses.
notes.assessment: 1. Migraine with aura — excellent response to topiramate 50 mg daily. Frequency reduced to ~1/month. 2. BRCA2 carrier — breast MRI (Nov 2023) negative. Mammogram due May 2024. Screening protocol on track. 3. Patient interested in genetic counseling follow-up regarding risk-reducing surgical options.
notes.plan: 1. Continue topiramate 50 mg daily — effective and well-tolerated. Continue sumatriptan PRN. 2. Schedule mammogram for May 2024. 3. Referral to Dr. Angela Torres, Genetic Counselor, Northwestern, for follow-up discussion on risk-reducing surgery vs continued surveillance. 4. Next annual physical June 2024.

referrals.to: Dr. Angela Torres, Genetic Counselor — Northwestern Medicine
referrals.reason: BRCA2 carrier follow-up: discussion of risk-reducing surgery options vs enhanced surveillance

encounters.reasonForVisit: Annual physical exam, BRCA2 screening protocol, annual labs

notes.subjective: Patient presents for annual physical. Feeling well. Migraines stable at approximately 1 per month on topiramate — excellent control. Reports she completed genetic counseling follow-up at Northwestern in March; discussed risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy and risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. She is 'still processing' and not ready to make a decision yet but found the session very informative. Mammogram May 2024 was negative. No new complaints. Sleeping well, exercising regularly, managing work stress through Pilates.
notes.objective: Well-appearing 41-year-old female in excellent overall health. HEENT normal. Heart RRR, no murmurs. Lungs CTA bilaterally. Breast exam: no masses, no skin changes, no nipple discharge, no lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, NT/ND. Skin exam: no concerning lesions. Neurological exam intact.
notes.assessment: 1. BRCA2 carrier — screening protocol adherent. Mammogram May 2024 BIRADS 1 (negative). Breast MRI due November 2024. Genetic counseling completed; patient considering risk-reducing options. 2. Migraine with aura — well controlled on topiramate 50 mg daily, ~1/month frequency. 3. Health maintenance: all screening up to date. Healthy BMI, excellent lifestyle habits.
notes.plan: 1. Continue enhanced screening: breast MRI November 2024, next mammogram May 2025. CA-125 ordered today. 2. Continue topiramate 50 mg daily and sumatriptan PRN. 3. Annual labs: CBC, CMP, lipid panel, CA-125. 4. Patient to continue considering risk-reducing surgery options; will revisit at next annual or sooner if desired. 5. Flu shot and COVID booster in fall. 6. Follow up 1 year or sooner as needed.

encounters.reasonForVisit: Breast MRI results review, migraine check-in

notes.subjective: Patient here to review breast MRI results (November 2024) and for migraine check. She reports the MRI was 'nerve-wracking as always' but she checked her portal and saw the results were normal. Migraines stable at 1/month. She mentions that her mother's 5-year remission anniversary is coming up in March and it has prompted her to think more seriously about prophylactic mastectomy. She is asking more specific questions about reconstruction options and recovery timelines. 'I need to understand what I'd be looking at in terms of time off work.'
notes.objective: Well-appearing, calm and composed. Vitals normal. Breast exam unremarkable. Reviewed breast MRI report: BIRADS 1, no suspicious findings.
notes.assessment: 1. BRCA2 carrier — breast MRI (Nov 2024) negative. All screening normal to date. Patient increasingly interested in risk-reducing surgery. 2. Migraine with aura — stable, well controlled.
notes.plan: 1. Breast MRI results reviewed and reassuring — BIRADS 1. Next mammogram May 2025. 2. Discussed risk-reducing mastectomy in more detail: reviewed lifetime risk (~45-55% for BRCA2), risk reduction with bilateral mastectomy (>95%), reconstruction options (implant-based vs autologous tissue), typical recovery 4-6 weeks. 3. Referred to Dr. Michelle Park, Breast Surgical Oncology, Northwestern, for surgical consultation if patient wishes to proceed. No pressure on timeline. 4. Continue current medications. 5. Follow up at annual physical June 2025.

referrals.to: Dr. Michelle Park, Breast Surgical Oncology — Northwestern Medicine
referrals.reason: BRCA2 carrier considering risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy; surgical consultation

encounters.reasonForVisit: Annual physical exam, BRCA2 screening review, annual labs

notes.subjective: Annual physical. Patient reports feeling well overall. Migraines remain at approximately 1/month — well controlled. She completed consultation with Dr. Park at Northwestern in April. She describes the meeting as 'really helpful' but says she is not ready to schedule surgery yet. She wants to continue surveillance for now and revisit the decision after turning 44. 'I want to make this decision from a place of clarity, not fear.' She completed her mammogram last week (May 2025), awaiting results. No new symptoms or complaints.
notes.objective: Well-appearing 42-year-old female in excellent health. HEENT normal. Thyroid non-palpable, no nodules. Heart RRR, no murmurs. Lungs CTA. Breast exam: no masses, no skin changes, no axillary lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, NT/ND. Extremities: no edema. Neurological exam normal. Skin: no concerning lesions. Mammogram (May 2025): BIRADS 1, negative.
notes.assessment: 1. BRCA2 carrier — mammogram May 2025 negative, BIRADS 1. All screening remains normal. Surgical consultation completed; patient electing continued surveillance with plan to reconsider after age 44. Next breast MRI November 2025. 2. Migraine with aura — well controlled on topiramate 50 mg daily, ~1/month. 3. Health maintenance: excellent. Healthy BMI, active lifestyle, no chronic disease.
notes.plan: 1. Continue enhanced screening protocol: breast MRI November 2025, mammogram May 2026. CA-125 ordered today for annual ovarian screening. 2. Continue topiramate 50 mg daily, sumatriptan 50 mg PRN. 3. Annual labs: CBC, CMP, lipid panel, CA-125. 4. Flu and COVID vaccines in fall. Tdap up to date. 5. Support patient's decision-making process regarding risk-reducing surgery — revisit at next annual or sooner. 6. Follow up 1 year or sooner as needed.

encounters.reasonForVisit: Migraine frequency increase — reported via portal message

notes.subjective: Patient presents after reporting increased migraine frequency via portal. Per her tracking, migraines increased from 1/month to 3/month over the past 2 months (December 2025 and January 2026). She has identified potential triggers: increased work stress (organizational restructuring at her company), disrupted sleep pattern, and possibly increased wine consumption over the holidays. Sumatriptan still effective for acute episodes. She is concerned about the regression and asks whether topiramate dose should be increased.
notes.objective: Alert, well-appearing, in no distress. BP slightly higher than baseline but within normal limits — likely stress-related. Neurological exam: normal cranial nerve function, no focal deficits, no papilledema. No signs of medication toxicity.
notes.assessment: Migraine with aura — frequency increased from 1/month to 3/month over past 2 months. Likely multifactorial: stress, sleep disruption, dietary triggers. Topiramate dose currently at 50 mg daily. No red flags on neurological exam.
notes.plan: 1. Reassurance: increased frequency likely related to identifiable stressors. 2. Optimize lifestyle triggers before medication adjustment: sleep hygiene counseling provided, discussed limiting red wine, stress management strategies. 3. Maintain topiramate at 50 mg daily for now — will reassess in 6-8 weeks. If frequency does not return to baseline, consider increasing to 75 mg. 4. Continue sumatriptan 50 mg PRN. 5. Patient to continue migraine diary. 6. Follow up in 6-8 weeks or sooner if worsening. Discussed with Dr. Kim.

messages.subject: BRCA2 screening schedule confirmation for 2025-2026
messages.body:
Dr. Kim,

I want to confirm my upcoming screening schedule is correct. Per our last conversation, I have the following on the calendar:

1. Breast MRI — November 2025 (referral to Northwestern Radiology)
2. Mammogram — May 2026 (annual)
3. CA-125 — completed June 2025 (normal, per my results)

Action items I need from your office:
- Please confirm the breast MRI referral is in place for November
- Please send the mammogram order to Northwestern Radiology when it's time (I'll schedule directly)

I also want to note that I have Dr. Park's surgical consultation on my calendar for a follow-up in early 2026, but that's a separate referral through Northwestern.

Thank you for keeping this organized with me.

Carmen Washington

thread.body:
Carmen,

Your screening schedule is exactly right. I've confirmed with our referral coordinator:

1. Breast MRI — referral is active with Northwestern Radiology for November 2025. You can call them directly to schedule: (312) 926-2000.
2. Mammogram — we'll send the order to Northwestern Radiology in April 2026 so you have time to schedule for May.
3. CA-125 — confirmed normal at 14 U/mL (June 2025). Next one due at your annual physical in June 2026.

Your follow-up with Dr. Park is a separate referral and still active — no action needed on our end.

You're doing an excellent job staying on top of this. Please don't hesitate to reach out if anything changes.

Best,
Dr. Kim

messages.subject: Migraine frequency increase — requesting appointment
messages.body:
Dr. Kim,

I'm writing to report a change in my migraine pattern and request an appointment to discuss.

Summary:
- Previous frequency: ~1 migraine/month (stable since starting topiramate Aug 2023)
- Current frequency: 3 migraines in December, 2 so far in January (as of 1/20)
- All episodes included visual aura followed by unilateral headache, consistent with my usual pattern

Potential triggers I've identified:
- Significant work stress (we're going through a company restructuring — I'm leading the HR side)
- Sleep disrupted: averaging 5-6 hours vs my usual 7-8
- Holiday period: increased wine consumption in December (more than my usual 3-4 glasses/week)

Current management:
- Sumatriptan 50mg still effective for acute episodes (relief within 60-90 min)
- Topiramate 50mg daily — taking consistently, no missed doses
- I've already cut wine back to 1 glass/week and am working on sleep hygiene

Questions:
1. Should we increase topiramate to 75mg?
2. At what point should I be concerned about this pattern?
3. Any other prophylactic strategies I should consider?

I'm available for an appointment any weekday before 9am or after 4pm. Please let me know what's available.

Thank you,
Carmen

thread.body:
Carmen,

Thank you for the detailed report — this is very helpful.

Given the clear trigger pattern (stress, sleep disruption, dietary), I think it's reasonable to address those factors first before adjusting medication. That said, I do want to see you to examine you and discuss this in person.

I've asked the front desk to schedule you for a follow-up. Rachel Torres, NP, has availability on February 10 at 8:00 AM — would that work? She and I work closely together and she'll consult me on any medication changes.

In the meantime:
- Continue topiramate 50mg daily
- Continue sumatriptan PRN
- Prioritize sleep — even 30 extra minutes can make a difference for migraine threshold
- You're smart to cut back on wine

To answer your questions briefly: I'd like to give lifestyle modification 6-8 weeks before increasing topiramate. The increase in frequency is concerning enough to monitor but not alarming given the identifiable triggers. We can discuss additional strategies at your visit.

Hang in there with the restructuring.

Dr. Kim

messages.subject: Referral request: genetic counselor follow-up (risk-reducing options)
messages.body:
Dr. Kim,

I'd like to request a follow-up referral to genetic counseling to discuss updated risk-reducing options. Here's my rationale:

- My last genetic counseling session was March 2024 (over a year ago)
- I've since completed my surgical consultation with Dr. Park (April 2025)
- New NCCN guidelines were published in January 2026 and I want to discuss any changes relevant to BRCA2 carriers
- My mother's oncologist recently recommended she have a conversation with me about risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy timing (she mentioned the recommended window is 35-45 for BRCA2)

I'm 43 now, so this feels time-sensitive — particularly the ovarian cancer risk discussion. I'd like to see someone at Northwestern if possible, ideally Dr. Torres (the genetic counselor I saw previously).

Requested action items:
1. Please submit referral to Dr. Angela Torres, Genetic Counseling, Northwestern
2. Please include my most recent screening results (mammogram, MRI, CA-125) with the referral
3. If there's a wait, I'd appreciate being put on a cancellation list

Thank you,
Carmen

thread.body:
Carmen,

Absolutely — this is a well-timed request. I agree that revisiting the conversation about risk-reducing options, particularly regarding ovarian cancer risk and the recommended timing for salpingo-oophorectomy in BRCA2 carriers, is important at your age.

I've submitted the referral today:
- Referral to: Dr. Angela Torres, Genetic Counseling, Northwestern Medicine
- Included: your full screening history (mammograms, breast MRIs, CA-125 results), genetic testing results, family history, and Dr. Park's surgical consultation notes
- I've noted your preference for the cancellation list if there's a wait

You should hear from Northwestern's scheduling office within 5-7 business days. If you don't, please call them directly at (312) 926-4500 and reference the referral.

Your mother's oncologist is right — the conversation about timing for risk-reducing BSO is important for BRCA2 carriers in your age range. I'm glad you're being proactive about this.

Please keep me posted on how the appointment goes.

Dr. Kim

messages.subject: Prophylactic mastectomy vs. surveillance — need your honest opinion
messages.body:
Dr. Kim,

I know this isn't really a portal question and I'm sorry for the length. I just got back from my genetic counseling follow-up and I need to process this with someone who knows my full picture.

Dr. Torres was wonderful. We went through everything — the updated risk models, the screening data, the surgical options, recovery timelines. I took notes. I made a spreadsheet. I did all the things I always do.

But here's the thing I can't put in a spreadsheet.

My grandmother was 52 when they found her breast cancer. She thought she was fine. She did her mammograms. And then at 61 it was ovarian, and by 63 she was gone. I was 17 when she died and I remember my mother — who was already going through her own treatment at that point — trying to hold it together at the funeral. I remember thinking: this is going to happen to me too.

I've been telling myself for years that I'm making this decision from a place of data and logic. But I'm 43 now. My grandmother was 52. That's nine years. And I keep doing the math.

I know the surveillance protocol is working. Everything has been negative. I know the data supports either approach. But I also know that my grandmother's screenings were "normal" too, until they weren't.

I'm not asking you to make this decision for me. But I need you to be honest with me — not as a doctor going through a checklist, but as someone who's been taking care of me for years. If I were your sister, what would you tell me?

I'm sorry this isn't my usual bullet-pointed message. I'll be more organized at my next appointment.

Carmen

thread.body:
Carmen,

Please don't apologize. This is exactly the kind of message I want you to feel comfortable sending me. The spreadsheets and bullet points are great — I mean that — but so is this.

First: you are not your grandmother. I know that's easy for me to say and harder for you to feel, but it matters. You've been in an enhanced screening program since age 30. You've had 13 years of normal mammograms and MRIs. Your grandmother didn't have access to BRCA2 testing, didn't have MRI screening, and wasn't being followed the way you are. The landscape is completely different.

But I also hear what you're saying, and you're not wrong to feel the urgency. So let me answer your actual question as honestly as I can.

If you were my sister, here's what I'd say:

1. The data supports both surveillance and risk-reducing surgery for BRCA2 carriers. Neither choice is wrong.

2. For the breasts: your current screening protocol is catching things early — that's what it's designed to do. Prophylactic bilateral mastectomy reduces breast cancer risk by over 95%. It's a significant surgery with real recovery, but many women in your situation find profound peace of mind afterward. If the anxiety of every screening cycle is eroding your quality of life, that matters too — it's not just about the numbers.

3. For the ovaries: this is where I'd be more direct. The data for risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy in BRCA2 carriers is strong, and the recommended window is 35-45. You're 43. Ovarian cancer screening (CA-125, even with ultrasound) is not as reliable as breast screening. Your grandmother's ovarian cancer is the part of your family history that concerns me most. I would encourage you to seriously consider BSO in the near term — not from fear, but because the risk-benefit profile is favorable and the window matters.

4. You don't have to do everything at once. Some patients do BSO first and continue breast surveillance. Some do both. There's no single right sequence.

Carmen, you have been one of the most thoughtful, proactive patients I've ever worked with. You've done everything right. Whatever you decide, you'll be making an informed choice — and I'll support you completely.

Let's schedule a longer appointment to talk this through in person. I'll have Maria block extra time. No bullet points required.

With care,
Dr. Kim

messages.subject: Prophylactic surgery discussion — next steps
messages.body:
Dr. Kim,

Thank you for your thoughtful response to my last message. It helped more than you know.

I've had time to process, and I'm ready to move forward with a structured conversation about next steps. I'd like to schedule a dedicated appointment to cover the following:

- **Prophylactic bilateral mastectomy vs. enhanced surveillance:** I want your clinical perspective on timing given my age (43) and family history. I've reviewed the literature but I want to talk through the decision framework with you specifically.

- **Risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy — now vs. deferred:** Your point about the BSO window was well taken. I need to understand the timeline, what deferral looks like in concrete risk terms, and the HRT implications (type, duration, risks given my BRCA2 status).

- **Referral coordination:** I'll need a breast surgeon, gynecologic oncologist, and possibly a second genetic counselor opinion. I'd like to discuss who you recommend and get those referrals started so I'm not waiting months for consultations.

- **Impact on migraine management:** I'm aware that surgical menopause from oophorectomy can significantly affect migraine patterns. Given that we just stabilized my migraines, I want to discuss how we'd manage that transition — whether topiramate dosing would need adjustment and what to expect.

Please block at least 30 minutes for this conversation. This isn't a 15-minute visit — I have specific questions for each topic and I want to leave with a clear action plan.

I'm available any weekday before 9:00 AM or after 4:00 PM. Please let me know what works.

Thank you,
Carmen
